title: Stelmark COO Defends Automation Push Amid Union Standoff
body:
Miriam Solano, who oversees operations at port automation firm Stelmark Freight, is at the center of one of the more contentious infrastructure fights on the Gulf Coast this year — and she's not backing down.

In an interview at Stelmark's Houston office last week, Solano said the company had gone out of its way to propose job retention provisions but that the ILA's demands would make the project economically unviable. "We can't operate half an automation system," she said. "The business case depends on scale."

Solano joined Stelmark in 2022 after a decade at a Dutch port consultancy. She is known in the industry for shepherding politically difficult projects through to completion, including a 2024 automated terminal expansion in Savannah that initially faced similar union opposition.

The Galveston dispute has attracted national attention, in part because the federal grant — funded through last year's infrastructure supplemental — was championed by several Texas legislators who are now getting calls from union constituents.

Washington lobbyist Renata Solis, who has advised Stelmark on federal relations, was seen leaving the Department of Transportation's headquarters on Tuesday, though neither she nor the DOT confirmed the nature of any meetings.

A final mediation session is scheduled for March 18. If no agreement is reached, the port authority says it will proceed with the Stelmark contract regardless.
